title: Diff Mode
subtitle: vimdiff and {key::diffthis} — visualize and edit changes side-by-side.
summary: Vim has a built-in diff view: `vimdiff a b` opens two files in vertical splits with synchronized scrolling and highlighted differences. ]c and [c jump between hunks; do and dp pull and push changes.

blocks.text: Vim has a real diff tool baked in. Launch with `vimdiff a b` from the shell, or `:diffsplit other.txt` to compare with the current buffer.

rows: Diff Obtain — pull change FROM the other file
Diff Put — push change TO the other file
Exit diff mode for current buffer

blocks.title: How Diff Mode Works
blocks.text:
Diff mode is a buffer-local *view* setting. When you `:diffthis` on a buffer, Vim:

  1. Marks the buffer as `'diff'`.
  2. Sets `'scrollbind'` and `'cursorbind'` so windows scroll together.
  3. Sets `'foldmethod=diff'` so unchanged regions fold away.
  4. Runs an external diff (default: the system `diff` program; `'diffexpr'` overrides) to find changed/added/removed lines.
  5. Highlights changes with the `DiffAdd`, `DiffChange`, `DiffDelete`, `DiffText` colorgroups.

The `vimdiff` shell command is just `vim -d` — opens files in vertical splits and runs `:diffthis` on each.

{key:d}{key:o} and {key:d}{key:p} actually edit buffers — they replace lines in one with lines from the other. Save afterwards to persist.
Comparing with the version on disk? `:w !diff % -` pipes the buffer to diff against the saved file.
